slides.title: JSON: The Web's Common Language
slides.body: **JSON** is a simple text format that every programming language understands. It's how Python talks to APIs, saves config files, and shares data with the world.
slides.tip: JSON looks almost identical to Python dicts — that's intentional!

slides.title: Python Types Map to JSON
slides.body: Python and JSON types match up almost perfectly. The key differences: JSON uses `true`/`false` (lowercase) and `null` instead of Python's `True`/`False`/`None`.
slides.code:
import json

json_str = '{"name": "Alice", "active": true, "score": null}'
data = json.loads(json_str)
print(data["active"], data["score"])
slides.tip: json.loads() = 'load from string' — the s stands for string!

slides.title: Python → JSON with dumps()
slides.body: `json.dumps()` converts a Python dict into a JSON **string**. Pass `indent=2` to make it human-readable instead of a single squashed line.
slides.tip: Notice False became false — Python and JSON differ on booleans.

slides.title: Read & Write JSON Files
slides.body: Use `json.load(f)` to read from a file and `json.dump(data, f)` to write to one. No `s` at the end = works with **f**iles, not strings.
slides.code:
import json

config = {"theme": "dark", "volume": 80}
with open("config.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(config, f, indent=2)

with open("config.json", "r") as f:
    loaded = json.load(f)
print(loaded["theme"])
slides.tip: With 's' → strings. Without 's' → files. That's the whole trick.

slides.title: You've Got JSON!
slides.body: Four functions, one simple rule. Use `loads`/`dumps` for strings, `load`/`dump` for files — and you can read APIs, write config files, and share data with any language.
slides.tip: Sets and custom objects can't be serialized directly — convert them to lists first.
